title: The Captain
content:
A LEGEND OF THE NAVY


He that only rules by terror
Doeth grievous wrong.
Deep as hell I count his error.
Let him hear my song.
Brave the Captain was; the seamen
Made a gallant crew,
Gallant sons of English freemen,
Sailors bold and true.
But they hated his oppression;
Stern he was and rash,
So for every light transgression
Doomï¿½d them to the lash.
Day by day more harsh and cruel
Seemï¿½d the Captainï¿½s mood.
Secret wrath like smotherï¿½d fuel
Burnt in each manï¿½s blood.
Yet he hoped to purchase glory,
Hoped to make the name
Of his vessel great in story,
Wheresoeï¿½er he came.
So they past by capes and islands,
Many a harbor-mouth,
Sailing under palmy highlands
Far within the South.
On a day when they were going
Oï¿½er the lone expanse,
In the north, her canvas flowing,
Rose a ship of France.
Then the Captainï¿½s color heightenï¿½d,
Joyful came his speech;
But a cloudy gladness lightenï¿½d
In the eyes of each.
ï¿½Chase,ï¿½ he said; the ship flew forward,
And the wind did blow;
Stately, lightly, went she norward,
Till she nearï¿½d the foe.
Then they lookï¿½d at him they hated,
Had what they desired;
Mute with folded arms they waitedï¿½
Not a gun was fired.
But they heard the foemanï¿½s thunder
Roaring out their doom;
All the air was torn in sunder,
Crashing went the boom,
Spars were splinterï¿½d, decks were shatterï¿½d,
Bullets fell like rain;
Over mast and deck were scatterï¿½d
Blood and brains of men.
Spars were splinterï¿½d; decks were broken;
Every motherï¿½s sonï¿½
Down they droptï¿½no word was spokenï¿½
Each beside his gun.
On the decks as they were lying,
Were their faces grim.
In their blood, as they lay dying,
Did they smile on him.
Those in whom he had reliance
For his noble name
With one smile of still defiance
Sold him unto shame.
Shame and wrath his heart confounded,
Pale he turnï¿½d and red,
Till himself was deadly wounded
Falling on the dead.
Dismal error! fearful slaughter!
Years have wanderï¿½d by;
Side by side beneath the water
Crew and Captain lie;
There the sunlit ocean tosses
Oï¿½er them mouldering,
And the lonely seabird crosses
With one waft of the wing.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Alfred Lord Tennyson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Alfred Lord Tennyson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.
